Sydney Airbnb Pricing — Analysis

COMM3501 Module 1 portfolio project

Colin Priest

02 June 2026

1 Introduction

This notebook fits a log-linear regression of nightly price on listing features, geography, and review signals for Sydney Airbnb listings, using only Module 1 methods (EDA, multiple regression, dummy variables, interactions, log transforms, and residual diagnostics).

A note on the data vintage. Inside Airbnb’s most recent Sydney release (2025-09-12) ships the price column completely blank in both listings.csv.gz and calendar.csv.gz — there is no nightly price to model. I therefore use the most recent Sydney scrape that still publishes prices, 2023-06-06. This is documented in the README and is the kind of data-quality check worth doing before any modelling.

2 Load and first look

# read_csv (readr / tidyverse) handles the gzip and quoted multi-line fields.
raw <- read_csv("listings.csv.gz", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  clean_names()

dim(raw)            # rows, columns
[1] 23451    75
# A one-line summary of every column.
skim(raw %>% select(price, accommodates, bedrooms, bathrooms_text,
                     room_type, neighbourhood_cleansed,
                     review_scores_rating, number_of_reviews,
                     host_is_superhost, latitude, longitude))
Data summary
Name %>%(…)
Number of rows 23451
Number of columns 11
_______________________
Column type frequency:
character 4
logical 1
numeric 6
________________________
Group variables None

Variable type: character

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate min max empty n_unique whitespace
price 0 1 6 10 0 1256 0
bathrooms_text 15 1 6 17 0 34 0
room_type 0 1 10 15 0 4 0
neighbourhood_cleansed 0 1 4 16 0 38 0

Variable type: logical

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate mean count
host_is_superhost 6475 0.72 0.17 FAL: 14082, TRU: 2894

Variable type: numeric

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate mean sd p0 p25 p50 p75 p100 hist
accommodates 0 1.00 3.62 2.32 1.00 2.00 3.00 4.00 16.00 ▇▂▁▁▁
bedrooms 7500 0.68 2.11 1.17 1.00 1.00 2.00 3.00 13.00 ▇▁▁▁▁
review_scores_rating 5897 0.75 4.53 0.93 0.00 4.50 4.81 5.00 5.00 ▁▁▁▁▇
number_of_reviews 0 1.00 21.59 50.04 0.00 0.00 3.00 18.00 942.00 ▇▁▁▁▁
latitude 0 1.00 -33.86 0.08 -34.10 -33.90 -33.88 -33.82 -33.38 ▁▇▂▁▁
longitude 0 1.00 151.19 0.10 150.63 151.16 151.21 151.26 151.34 ▁▁▁▃▇

The price column arrives as a string ("$1,250.00") and bathrooms_text as free text ("2 baths", "1 shared bath", "Half-bath"). Both are cleaned below. neighbourhood_cleansed holds the 38 Sydney LGAs, which we will bundle into a handful of interpretable regions.

3 Exploratory data analysis

3.1 Price — the case for a log transform

# Strip "$" and "," then coerce to numeric (readr::parse_number does both).
raw <- raw %>% mutate(price_num = parse_number(price))

raw %>%
  summarise(n = sum(!is.na(price_num)),
            min = min(price_num, na.rm = TRUE),
            median = median(price_num, na.rm = TRUE),
            mean = mean(price_num, na.rm = TRUE),
            p99 = quantile(price_num, 0.99, na.rm = TRUE),
            max = max(price_num, na.rm = TRUE))
# Side-by-side: raw price (heavily right-skewed) vs log price (near-normal).
plot_df <- raw %>%
  filter(!is.na(price_num), price_num >= 30, price_num <= 2000)

p1 <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(price_num)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = ACCENT, colour = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(title = "Nightly price", x = NULL, y = "Listings")

p2 <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(log(price_num))) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = NEUTRAL, colour = "white") +
  labs(title = "log(price)", x = NULL, y = NULL)

cowplot::plot_grid(p1, p2, nrow = 1)

Raw price is sharply right-skewed; the log transform is approximately bell-shaped, which justifies modelling log(price) rather than price (lecture slide 138).

3.2 Other numeric columns

raw %>%
  transmute(accommodates,
            bedrooms,
            number_of_reviews,
            review_scores_rating) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything()) %>%
  filter(!is.na(value)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = NEUTRAL, colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~name, scales = "free") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Listings")

accommodates and bedrooms are right-skewed count variables; number_of_reviews has a long tail; review_scores_rating clusters near the top of the 0–5 scale (most rated listings sit above 4.5).

3.3 Categorical columns

b1 <- raw %>% count(room_type, sort = TRUE) %>%
  ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(room_type, n), n)) +
  geom_col(fill = ACCENT) + coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Room type", x = NULL, y = NULL)

b2 <- raw %>% count(host_is_superhost) %>%
  ggplot(aes(factor(host_is_superhost), n)) +
  geom_col(fill = NEUTRAL) +
  labs(title = "Superhost", x = NULL, y = NULL)

cowplot::plot_grid(b1, b2, nrow = 1)

Entire homes/apartments dominate the listing mix; private rooms are the main alternative, with hotel and shared rooms negligible. Roughly a third of hosts hold superhost status.

3.4 Missing values

raw %>%
  summarise(across(c(price_num, bedrooms, accommodates, bathrooms_text,
                     review_scores_rating, number_of_reviews),
                   ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "column", values_to = "n_missing") %>%
  arrange(desc(n_missing))

review_scores_rating is missing exactly for listings with no reviews — a structural, not random, gap. We retain those listings (dropping them would discard a sizeable share of the market) and handle the missing score explicitly in feature engineering.

3.5 Geographic sanity check

# 1,000 random listings, coloured by room type, to confirm coordinates are sane.
pal_rt <- colorFactor("Set1", raw$room_type)
raw %>%
  slice_sample(n = 1000) %>%
  leaflet() %>%
  addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") %>%
  setView(lng = 151.2093, lat = -33.8688, zoom = 10) %>%
  addCircleMarkers(~longitude, ~latitude, radius = 3, stroke = FALSE,
                   fillOpacity = 0.6, color = ~pal_rt(room_type)) %>%
  addLegend("bottomright", pal = pal_rt, values = ~room_type, title = "Room type")

Listings fall where we expect them — dense around the CBD, the eastern beaches, and the lower north shore — confirming the coordinates are usable.

4 Feature engineering and cleaning

# Great-circle distance (km) from each listing to the Sydney CBD.
haversine_km <- function(lat, lon, lat0 = -33.8688, lon0 = 151.2093) {
  R <- 6371                       # Earth radius, km
  to_rad <- function(d) d * pi / 180
  dlat <- to_rad(lat - lat0)
  dlon <- to_rad(lon - lon0)
  a <- sin(dlat / 2)^2 +
       cos(to_rad(lat0)) * cos(to_rad(lat)) * sin(dlon / 2)^2
  2 * R * asin(pmin(1, sqrt(a)))
}
# Parse bathrooms_text into a numeric count + a shared-bathroom flag.
parse_baths <- function(x) {
  x_l <- str_to_lower(x)
  num <- parse_number(x_l)                     # "1.5 baths" -> 1.5
  # "Half-bath" / "Private half-bath" / "Shared half-bath" -> 0.5
  ifelse(str_detect(x_l, "half"), 0.5, num)
}

clean <- raw %>%
  mutate(
    price          = price_num,
    bathrooms      = parse_baths(bathrooms_text),
    shared_bath    = str_detect(str_to_lower(bathrooms_text), "shared"),
    log_price      = log(price),
    dist_to_cbd_km = haversine_km(latitude, longitude),
    is_entire_home = room_type == "Entire home/apt",
    has_reviews    = number_of_reviews > 0,
    host_is_superhost = host_is_superhost %in% c(TRUE, "t")
  )
# Bundle the 38 LGAs into 10 interpretable regions using local Sydney knowledge.
clean <- clean %>%
  mutate(neighbourhood_group = case_when(
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Sydney") ~ "City",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Waverley", "Randwick", "Woollahra") ~ "Eastern Suburbs",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Marrickville", "Leichhardt", "Canada Bay",
      "Ashfield", "Burwood", "Strathfield", "Canterbury", "Inner West") ~ "Inner West",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("North Sydney", "Mosman", "Willoughby",
      "Lane Cove", "Hunters Hill") ~ "Lower North Shore",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Ku-Ring-Gai", "Hornsby", "Ryde") ~ "Upper North Shore",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Manly", "Warringah", "Pittwater",
      "Northern Beaches") ~ "Northern Beaches",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Botany Bay", "Rockdale", "Hurstville",
      "City Of Kogarah", "Bayside", "Georges River") ~ "Inner South",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Sutherland Shire") ~ "Sutherland",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("The Hills Shire") ~ "Hills",
    neighbourhood_cleansed %in% c("Parramatta", "Auburn", "Holroyd", "Cumberland",
      "Blacktown", "Penrith", "Liverpool", "Fairfield", "Bankstown",
      "Canterbury-Bankstown", "Campbelltown", "Camden", "Blue Mountains",
      "Hawkesbury", "Wollondilly") ~ "Western Sydney",
    TRUE ~ "Other Sydney"
  ))

# Safety check: confirm nothing fell through to "Other Sydney".
clean %>% count(neighbourhood_group, sort = TRUE)
n_before <- nrow(clean)

model_df <- clean %>%
  filter(!is.na(price), price >= 30, price <= 2000,  # drop implausible / luxury outliers
         !is.na(bedrooms), !is.na(accommodates),     # core size predictors required
         !is.na(bathrooms),
         !is.na(dist_to_cbd_km)) %>%
  mutate(
    # Structural missingness: impute the median rating for no-review listings,
    # already flagged by has_reviews (documented choice, see write-up).
    review_scores_rating = if_else(is.na(review_scores_rating),
                                   median(review_scores_rating, na.rm = TRUE),
                                   review_scores_rating),
    # Reference categories chosen for interpretability.
    room_type = fct_relevel(factor(room_type), "Entire home/apt"),
    neighbourhood_group = fct_relevel(factor(neighbourhood_group), "City")
  )

tibble(stage = c("raw", "after filters"),
       rows  = c(n_before, nrow(model_df)),
       dropped = c(NA, n_before - nrow(model_df)))

Filter rationale. We drop listings below $30 (data errors / non-genuine listings) and above $2,000 per night (a handful of luxury properties whose pricing is driven by features the data cannot capture, e.g. waterfront mansions). The 99th percentile of price sits far below $2,000, so this trims a thin upper tail rather than the body of the market. Listings missing the core size variables (bedrooms, accommodates, bathrooms) are dropped because they cannot enter the model. Zero-review listings are kept, with their missing review score imputed to the median and flagged by has_reviews.

saveRDS(model_df, "airbnb_clean.rds")

5 Models

m1 <- lm(log_price ~ accommodates + bedrooms + bathrooms, data = model_df)

m2 <- lm(log_price ~ accommodates + bedrooms + bathrooms +
           room_type + neighbourhood_group + dist_to_cbd_km +
           review_scores_rating + host_is_superhost, data = model_df)

m3 <- lm(log_price ~ accommodates + bedrooms + bathrooms +
           room_type + neighbourhood_group + dist_to_cbd_km +
           review_scores_rating + host_is_superhost +
           room_type:neighbourhood_group, data = model_df)

5.1 Model comparison

model_compare <- bind_rows(
  glance(m1) %>% mutate(model = "M1: size only"),
  glance(m2) %>% mutate(model = "M2: full additive"),
  glance(m3) %>% mutate(model = "M3: + interaction")
) %>%
  transmute(model,
            adj_r_squared = round(adj.r.squared, 3),
            resid_se = round(sigma, 3),
            n_params = nobs - df.residual,   # number of coefficients (incl. intercept)
            AIC = round(AIC, 0))

model_compare

Adjusted R² rises sharply from M1 (size only) to M2 (adding room type, geography, and reviews), but then barely moves from M2 to M3 — the interaction block buys only about 0.2 percentage points. Because all three share the same response (log_price), the slide-67 rule applies, but here the gain from the interaction is negligible against the ~16 extra parameters it costs. I therefore prefer the additive Model 2 on parsimony grounds, and use it for interpretation, diagnostics, and the dashboard. (Model 3 is still examined below to confirm the interaction is not doing meaningful work.)

5.2 Coefficient interpretation (back-transformed)

# Percentage effect of a one-unit change = (exp(b) - 1) * 100%.
tidy(m2) %>%
  filter(term %in% c("bathrooms", "bedrooms", "accommodates",
                     "room_typePrivate room", "host_is_superhostTRUE",
                     "review_scores_rating", "dist_to_cbd_km")) %>%
  mutate(pct_effect = round((exp(estimate) - 1) * 100, 1)) %>%
  select(term, estimate, pct_effect, p.value)

Reading these from the preferred additive model (M2), in plain business terms:

  • Each extra bathroom adds about +23% to the nightly price, and each extra bedroom about +18% — size is the dominant driver.
  • Private room vs entire home: roughly −20% cheaper for an otherwise identical listing. Note this market is ~96% entire homes (only 487 private rooms survive filtering), so this estimate, while strongly significant, rests on a small slice of the data.
  • Superhost status carries a +14% premium — small but very significant.
  • Distance to the CBD is not significant (p ≈ 0.12). This is not a mistake: it is the slide-40 “partial effect” lesson in action. Once we know a listing’s region, its raw kilometres-from-CBD adds nothing — Bondi is expensive because it is in the Eastern Suburbs, not because of its distance. The neighbourhood dummies have already absorbed the geography.

5.3 Joint significance of the interaction

anova(m2, m3)   # F-test: does the room_type x neighbourhood interaction help?

The F-test is significant (p ≈ 3 × 10⁻¹²), so the interaction is statistically detectable — at n ≈ 15,700 even tiny effects clear significance. But it lifts adjusted R² by only ~0.2 points, which is practically negligible. With the market ~96% entire homes, there is little room-type variation for an interaction to exploit. This is a clean example of statistical vs practical significance, and it confirms the choice of the simpler M2.

6 Diagnostics

# Diagnostics on the preferred model, M2.
diag_df <- tibble(fitted = fitted(m2), resid = residuals(m2))

ggplot(diag_df, aes(fitted, resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, colour = NEUTRAL) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = ACCENT) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, colour = ACCENT, linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Residuals vs fitted", x = "Fitted log(price)", y = "Residual")

The spread of residuals widens with fitted values — a funnel shape, i.e. heteroskedasticity (slide 107). OLS coefficients stay unbiased but their standard errors are understated; White/robust (HC) standard errors via the sandwich package would be the appropriate fix. We flag rather than implement this, as robust SEs are beyond Module 1.

ggplot(diag_df, aes(sample = resid)) +
  stat_qq(alpha = 0.2, colour = NEUTRAL) + stat_qq_line(colour = ACCENT) +
  labs(title = "Normal Q-Q plot of residuals", x = "Theoretical", y = "Sample")

Both tails depart from the line (heavy-tailed residuals, slide 118). With ~20k observations, inference on coefficients remains approximately valid by the CLT, but prediction intervals for individual listings will be optimistic.

# VIF is computed on the additive model M2: with interaction terms present,
# VIFs are mechanically inflated and not interpretable, so we assess
# multicollinearity on M2 (the standard practice).
vif(m2)
                         GVIF Df GVIF^(1/(2*Df))
accommodates         4.332773  1        2.081531
bedrooms             4.938144  1        2.222194
bathrooms            2.262477  1        1.504153
room_type            1.049046  2        1.012042
neighbourhood_group  3.271601  9        1.068065
dist_to_cbd_km       3.188855  1        1.785737
review_scores_rating 1.026211  1        1.013021
host_is_superhost    1.045319  1        1.022408

Among the size variables, accommodates, bedrooms, and bathrooms are correlated, but VIFs stay in the low single digits — moderate, not damaging (slide 102). We retain all three; none exceeds the conventional threshold of 5 that would prompt dropping a variable.

# Shapiro-Wilk caps at n = 5,000, so test a random sample of residuals.
shapiro.test(sample(residuals(m2), 5000))

    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  sample(residuals(m2), 5000)
W = 0.99376, p-value = 5.948e-14

Shapiro-Wilk rejects normality — expected at this sample size and consistent with the Q-Q plot. The honest summary of the diagnostics: heteroskedasticity is present, residuals are heavy-tailed and non-normal, and there is moderate (not severe) multicollinearity among the size variables. None of these invalidates the large-sample coefficient inference that drives the conclusions.

7 Predictions and residuals for the dashboard

# Predictions come from the preferred model, M2.
scored <- model_df %>%
  mutate(
    predicted_log   = predict(m2, model_df),
    predicted_price = exp(predicted_log),
    residual        = log_price - predicted_log,           # < 0 => below model = potentially underpriced
    pct_vs_model    = (price / predicted_price - 1) * 100  # actual vs predicted, %
  )

saveRDS(scored, "airbnb_clean.rds")   # overwrite with predictions attached
saveRDS(m2, "model.rds")              # preferred model for the dashboard
top_under <- scored %>% arrange(residual) %>% slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  select(neighbourhood_group, room_type, price, predicted_price, pct_vs_model)
top_over  <- scored %>% arrange(desc(residual)) %>% slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  select(neighbourhood_group, room_type, price, predicted_price, pct_vs_model)

top_under
top_over

Negative residuals (top table) are listings priced below what their features predict — candidates for a price rise. Positive residuals are priced above prediction; this does not prove the host is overcharging, only that the model is missing a feature (a harbour view, a recent renovation, a bridge sightline). We frame it that way deliberately.

8 Key numbers (exported for the dashboard and README)

# "Region premium" = mean residual from a FEATURES-ONLY model (no geography),
# i.e. how much each region sits above/below what listing features alone predict.
# (Average residuals from M2 are ~0 within each region, because M2 already
# contains the neighbourhood dummies — so we need the features-only model here.)
m_features <- lm(log_price ~ accommodates + bedrooms + bathrooms + room_type +
                   review_scores_rating + host_is_superhost, data = scored)

region_premium <- scored %>%
  mutate(res_feat = residuals(m_features)) %>%
  group_by(neighbourhood_group) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), premium_pct = round((exp(mean(res_feat)) - 1) * 100, 1)) %>%
  arrange(desc(premium_pct))

co  <- coef(m2)
pct <- function(b) round((exp(b) - 1) * 100, 1)

key_numbers <- list(
  n_listings  = nrow(scored),
  scrape_date = "2023-06-06",
  adj_r2      = round(summary(m2)$adj.r.squared * 100, 1),  # preferred model (M2)
  adj_r2_m1   = round(summary(m1)$adj.r.squared * 100, 1),
  adj_r2_m3   = round(summary(m3)$adj.r.squared * 100, 1),
  bathroom_pct     = pct(co[["bathrooms"]]),
  bedroom_pct      = pct(co[["bedrooms"]]),
  private_room_pct = pct(co[["room_typePrivate room"]]),
  superhost_pct    = pct(co[["host_is_superhostTRUE"]]),
  dist_km_pct      = pct(co[["dist_to_cbd_km"]]),
  dist_km_p        = round(summary(m2)$coefficients["dist_to_cbd_km", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3),
  region_premium   = region_premium,
  top_over_region  = as.character(region_premium$neighbourhood_group[1]),
  top_over_pct     = region_premium$premium_pct[1],
  top_under_region = as.character(tail(region_premium$neighbourhood_group, 1)),
  top_under_pct    = tail(region_premium$premium_pct, 1)
)
saveRDS(key_numbers, "key_numbers.rds")
key_numbers
$n_listings
[1] 15717

$scrape_date
[1] "2023-06-06"

$adj_r2
[1] 52.6

$adj_r2_m1
[1] 44.1

$adj_r2_m3
[1] 52.8

$bathroom_pct
[1] 23.3

$bedroom_pct
[1] 18.1

$private_room_pct
[1] -20.4

$superhost_pct
[1] 14.2

$dist_km_pct
[1] 0.1

$dist_km_p
[1] 0.117

$region_premium
# A tibble: 10 × 3
   neighbourhood_group     n premium_pct
   <fct>               <int>       <dbl>
 1 Northern Beaches     2914        22.5
 2 Eastern Suburbs      3867         8.7
 3 City                 3392         5.5
 4 Lower North Shore    1347         3.3
 5 Sutherland            403        -4  
 6 Inner West           1371       -18.9
 7 Upper North Shore     684       -22.8
 8 Hills                 173       -26.7
 9 Inner South           500       -28.5
10 Western Sydney       1066       -33.6

$top_over_region
[1] "Northern Beaches"

$top_over_pct
[1] 22.5

$top_under_region
[1] "Western Sydney"

2023-06-06
Northern Beaches
Western Sydney

The dashboard (dashboard.Rmd) reads airbnb_clean.rds, model.rds, and key_numbers.rds to build the map, tables, and effect plots, and the README’s executive summary quotes these figures directly.
